title: U.S.-backed chip startup xLight aims to raise $350 million for an EUV alternative
gist: U.S.-backed startup xLight, chaired by former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger, is raising $350 million from Boardman Bay Capital and Bain Capital to develop free-electron laser-based EUV alternatives to ASML's machines. The U.S. government has already invested $150 million via the CHIPS Act, and xLight has secured $4.2 billion in non-binding project financing with a first prototype targeted for 2028.

themes.theme: U.S. Strategic Push to Break ASML EUV Monopoly

insights.claim: U.S.-backed startup xLight is raising $350 million led by Boardman Bay Capital Management and Bain Capital to develop free-electron laser-based EUV lithography alternatives to ASML's laser-produced plasma technology, with the U.S. government already holding a $150 million CHIPS Act stake.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Government-backed competition to ASML's EUV monopoly, if successful, would structurally alter semiconductor equipment pricing and supply chain geopolitics.
insights.quote: The U.S. government has invested $150 million in xLight through the CHIPS and Science Act, bringing xLight's total funding to approximately $200 million.

insights.claim: Former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger serves as executive chairman of xLight and his firm Playground Global invested in the company in 2025; xLight is lobbying ASML, TSMC, Intel, and Micron to participate in its $350 million funding round.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Gelsinger's involvement and the effort to recruit ASML and TSMC as co-investors signals an attempt to create industry alignment against ASML's pricing power.
insights.quote: Former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger serves as executive chairman of xLight, and his firm, Playground Global, invested in xLight in 2025.

insights.claim: ASML CEO Christophe Fouquet confirmed ASML is collaborating with xLight on technology demonstrations, even as xLight aims to build an alternative to ASML's core EUV product.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: ASML's collaboration with a direct competitor is paradoxical; it may reflect regulatory pressure or hedging, but undermines the threat narrative around xLight.
insights.quote: ASML CEO Christophe Fouquet revealed that ASML is collaborating with xLight on technology demonstrations.

insights.claim: xLight has signed non-binding project financing agreements worth $4.2 billion with lenders to build its facilities, with the first prototype light source targeted for 2028 and the first prototype site planned for Albany, New York.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A 2028 prototype target with only non-binding financing means commercialization is at minimum 5-7 years away, limiting near-term investment thesis but establishing a long-duration option on domestic EUV capability.
insights.quote: xLight has signed nonbinding agreements securing up to $4.2 billion in project financing to build its facilities, with the first prototype being developed in Albany, New York. The first working light source is targeted for 2028.

insights.claim: Substrate (a separate company) is also developing X-ray lithography technology, directly competing with ASML's EUV approach, indicating a broader ecosystem of U.S. alternatives to ASML is emerging.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Multiple competing alternative lithography bets signals genuine government and investor conviction in breaking the ASML monopoly, increasing the probability that at least one alternative reaches commercialization.
insights.quote: Substrate is also developing X-ray lithography technology, directly competing with ASML.
